title: Oscar-Nominated Sentimental Value Editor Olivier Bugge Coutté Breaks Down the Film's Most Devastating Scenes
text: Danish editor Olivier Bugge Coutté discussed his work on Joachim Trier's acclaimed film Sentimental Value, which earned nine Oscar nominations including one for Coutté's editing. This marks Coutté's sixth collaboration with director Trier. The editor explained their working relationship: We're friends who have known each other for 30 years, and our families know each other. So, it's very natural. He compared their partnership to playing in a band—when he comes back to this band, he knows exactly what kind of music they're playing. Cinematographer Kasper Tuxen created an innovative morphing sequence where the faces of Gustav, Nora, and Agnes blend into one another—all captured in-camera without digital tricks. The technique involves rewinding film and re-exposing the same shot with different lighting for each actor. A pivotal scene contrasts Rachel Kemp's English-language reading of Gustav's screenplay with Nora's Norwegian version of the identical text. Rachel's performance, while technically skilled, differs from Nora's more internalized reaction: It hits her on a personal level, whereas it doesn't hit Rachel the same way. The greatest difficulty involved balancing the four characters against each other, requiring millimeter by millimeter by millimeter work.
